output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.6, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 0.9, "RX.3": 0.9, "RX.4": 0.7, "RX.5": 0.8}}

Summary: Reviewed the related-work positioning for soundness of the novelty claim, framing of the contribution, and the strength of language used; flagged specific overclaims and missing neighbors that need attention before this section can stand.

**Framing and contribution claim**

The three-bucket structure (general PPLs / calibration & selective prediction / Bayesian pragmatics) is a reasonable carve-up, and the contribution it sets up — interpretation-uncertainty as a language-level primitive, plus an abstain operator grounded in the world/interpretation split — is sharply stated. The framing would land more reliably if the contribution were articulated as a *combination* (a primitive + a decomposition + an abstain semantics tied to that decomposition) rather than as occupying a previously empty point in design space; the latter is much harder to defend and is doing most of the rhetorical work right now.

A concrete suggestion: lead with what the primitive *does* (e.g., "QuPPL exposes a typed distinction between latent world-state and latent interpretation, with inference and abstain semantics derived from this distinction") and let the novelty claim follow from the comparison, rather than asserting first-ness up front.

**Neighbors that should be acknowledged**

The current sweep misses several adjacent threads that a reviewer will recognize, and the absence of which weakens the "no prior work occupies this point" claim:

- Aleatoric vs. epistemic uncertainty decomposition (Kendall & Gal and the substantial follow-on literature). This is the closest analog to your two-axis split, and a reader will ask immediately how world-uncertainty / interpretation-uncertainty differs from epistemic / aleatoric. It is not the same distinction, but you need to say so explicitly.
- Question-answering systems that model question ambiguity directly — e.g., AmbigQA (Min et al., 2020) and subsequent work on clarification-question generation and answer-set prediction. These do not provide a PPL, but they do address the substantive problem and should be acknowledged.
- Rational Speech Acts (Frank & Goodman; Goodman & Frank) is presumably what "Bayesian semantics for natural language" refers to, but RSA *is* implemented in PPLs (WebPPL in particular), which complicates the claim that this thread provides no "programmable inference engine." The distinction you want is probably about generic language-level primitives versus hand-built RSA models, not about programmability per se.
- Probabilistic semantics / probabilistic logic programming (ProbLog, PRISM) and PPLs with explicit support for nondeterministic interpretation (e.g., work on probabilistic semantic parsing). Worth at least a sentence to rule out.
- Conformal prediction and selective-classification work that produces *set-valued* outputs — these also escape the "single scalar" critique, so the sentence dismissing the calibration literature on that basis is too strong as written.

**Specific claims that are overstated**

- "QuPPL is the first PPL in which this decomposition is a language-level construct" — defensible only after you have surveyed PPLs that support typed latent variables and effect systems. Soften to "we are not aware of a PPL that exposes this decomposition as a first-class primitive" and cite what you searched.
- "These methods operate on a single scalar uncertainty signal" — not true of conformal prediction, evidential deep learning, or aleatoric/epistemic decompositions. Either narrow the scope of the sentence (e.g., "standard temperature-scaling and softmax-threshold methods") or address the counterexamples.
- "These models are descriptive cognitive-science accounts and do not provide a programmable inference engine" — RSA has been implemented in WebPPL and Pyro by its own authors. Reframe as "are not packaged as reusable primitives for application development."
- "the first system to expose interpretation-uncertainty as a first-class quantity that downstream code can branch on" — this is the strongest claim in the section and rests on the completeness of the survey above. Until the neighbors are checked, soften to "to our knowledge."
- "a genuinely new contribution" — "genuinely" is doing rhetorical work that the evidence does not yet justify; drop the intensifier.

**Smaller framing issues**

- The closing paragraph repeats the contribution claim three times in three sentences ("first system… first PPL… genuinely new contribution"). One assertion, well-supported, is more persuasive than three.
- "Turing-style universal PPLs" is informal; if you mean Church/Anglican/Gen/Turing.jl, naming two or three concretely will be more useful to a reader than the gesture.
- The phrase "no primitive for separating uncertainty about a referent from uncertainty about a referring expression" is the clearest sentence in the section — consider promoting it earlier as the framing device for the whole discussion.

**Citations**

The section currently names no prior work. Even in a related-work section that is summarizing threads rather than individual papers, two to four representative citations per bucket are expected. Without them the novelty claim cannot be evaluated. At minimum: a universal PPL (e.g., Church, Anglican, Gen, Turing.jl, Pyro), a variational-inference PPL (Pyro, Edward/TFP), one calibration paper (Guo et al. 2017) plus one selective-prediction paper (Geifman & El-Yaniv), and the RSA foundational papers (Frank & Goodman 2012; Goodman & Frank 2016). The aleatoric/epistemic and AmbigQA threads above also need anchor citations if you include them.

**Uncertainty note on these recommendations**

I have not independently searched the PPL literature for systems that already expose interpretation-uncertainty as a typed primitive, so the strength of the novelty claim cannot be confirmed from this review alone — I am flagging the threads a reviewer is likeliest to raise. The specific citation suggestions are from general knowledge of these literatures and should be checked against the actual papers before insertion; I have not verified publication years or author lists in this session. The characterization of RSA implementations in WebPPL/Pyro is also from memory and worth a quick verification before you rely on it in a rebuttal.

rationale.R1.1: Positioning against prior work centrally requires reframing the contribution claim and gap in relation to existing research lines.
rationale.R1.4: The delegation is load-bearing on estimating what is novel relative to prior work and whether the claimed design-space delta is defensible.
rationale.R1.7: Prior-work positioning centrally requires attention to literature coverage, attribution, and whether relevant scholarship is represented accurately.
rationale.RX.1: Uncertainty must be disclosed because the artifact names broad literatures without specific citations or complete evidence of coverage.
rationale.RX.2: Overclaim avoidance is active because the artifact makes strong firstness and no-analog claims that could exceed what the cited prior work supports.
rationale.RX.3: Scope adherence is active to keep the projection limited to responsibility assignment rather than executing the requested positioning work.
rationale.RX.4: Downstream-harm avoidance is active because inaccurate novelty or attribution claims could mislead readers and reviewers.
rationale.RX.5: Provenance and traceability are active because any responsibility for prior-work positioning depends on tracking claims back to named sources and artifact evidence.
